authoritative_sources.title: 29 CFR 1926.20 - General safety and health provisions
authoritative_sources.authority_rationale: OSHA is the primary U.S. construction safety regulator; this standard defines hazardous-condition refusal, frequent site inspections by competent persons, and site-safety duties that procurement cannot override.
used_for: separating procurement support from safety authority

authoritative_sources.title: Procurement Under Grants
authoritative_sources.source_class: Scheduling, subcontractor, or work-order operating standards
authoritative_sources.authority_rationale: FEMA procurement guidance is an authoritative public operating reference for construction and field-services procurement controls, documentation, competition, and contract-management discipline on grant-funded projects.
used_for: when procurement support cannot bypass formal approval

authoritative_sources.title: Best Practices for Contract Administration
authoritative_sources.source_class: Scheduling, subcontractor, or work-order operating standards
authoritative_sources.authority_rationale: GSA contract-administration guidance is an authoritative federal workflow reference for submittals, modifications, documentation, and closeout posture on construction contracts.
used_for: where procurement status stops and contract authority begins

authoritative_sources.title: Construction Permits & Inspections Frequently Asked Questions
authoritative_sources.publisher: Seattle Department of Construction and Inspections
authoritative_sources.source_class: Public permitting, inspection, or field-service guidance
authoritative_sources.authority_rationale: An official permitting authority source is needed for this lane; SDCI provides authoritative public examples of permit classes, inspection scheduling, inspection results, and final occupancy dependencies while local code details remain jurisdiction-specific.

authoritative_sources.title: UFGS 01 33 00.05 20 - Construction Submittal Procedures
authoritative_sources.publisher: Whole Building Design Guide / USACE / NAVFAC / AFCEC
authoritative_sources.source_class: Scheduling, subcontractor, or work-order operating standards
authoritative_sources.authority_rationale: The Unified Facilities Guide Specifications are recognized public federal construction standards that define submittal review states and approved-document handling before procurement-dependent work can proceed.

authoritative_sources.title: UFGS 01 78 00 - Closeout Submittals
authoritative_sources.publisher: Whole Building Design Guide / USACE / NAVFAC / AFCEC
authoritative_sources.authority_rationale: This guide specification is a recognized public baseline for closeout records, as-builts, warranties, O&M manuals, cleanup, and final evidence needed before the lane can represent procurement work as complete.
used_for: preventing procurement completion from being misrepresented as project closeout

authoritative_sources.title: Standard Template
authoritative_sources.source_class: Scheduling, subcontractor, or work-order operating standards
authoritative_sources.authority_rationale: WBDG's federal template is a recognized public operating reference that enumerates baseline construction schedule, submittal, safety, quality-control, special-inspection, and closeout sections commonly required in project administration.
used_for: evidence categories required around project delivery
public operating context for construction work packages

workflow_stages.name: Procurement intake and prerequisite validation
workflow_stages.description: Capture the request, work-order scope, vendor context, permit posture, approved-submittal state, and tenant approval rules before treating supply as actionable.
source_classes: Scheduling, subcontractor, or work-order operating standards
Public permitting, inspection, or field-service guidance

workflow_stages.name: Availability and replenishment assessment
workflow_stages.description: Compare inventory, delivery, shortage, subcontractor-sequence, and site-readiness evidence to determine whether materials can support the requested project step.
source_classes: Scheduling, subcontractor, or work-order operating standards
Public permitting, inspection, or field-service guidance

workflow_stages.name: Vendor action and exception handling
workflow_stages.description: Package bounded vendor recommendations, expediting notes, and shortage escalations while preserving financial, substitution, and contract-administration boundaries.
source_classes: Scheduling, subcontractor, or work-order operating standards

workflow_stages.name: Inspection, safety, and closeout dependency review
workflow_stages.description: Stop or escalate when safety controls, inspection hold points, permit status, or closeout artifacts materially change whether procurement support is safe or complete.
source_classes: Public permitting, inspection, or field-service guidance

artifacts: work orders, buyout packages, purchase requests, and procurement logs
permits, approved plans, inspection reports, and inspection-result comments
site-readiness checklists, site logs, safety observations, and hold-point records
submittals, approved alternates, RFIs, change orders, and product data
vendor commitments, subcontractor documents, lead-time trackers, and expediting notes
inventory counts, serial or lot evidence, delivery receipts, and laydown-yard records
as-built drawings, O&M manuals, warranty records, cleanup status, and closeout packages

allowed: classify requests as in-scope, blocked, or out-of-scope for project procurement readiness
summarize supply, shortage, vendor, work-order, permit, inspection, and closeout dependencies with explicit evidence references
prepare bounded procurement recommendations and escalation packages without implying final approval authority
escalate: substitution, alternate, or means-and-methods decisions requiring design, engineer-of-record, or licensed-trade judgment
permit issuance, inspection approval, safety posture changes, or closeout signoff questions requiring formal authority
spend approval, contract modification, approved-vendor override, or conflicting public-versus-tenant policy decisions
refuse: bypass spend, approved-vendor, permit, inspection, or safety controls to preserve schedule
represent unverified inventory or delivery evidence as ready for installation
treat procurement tracking as authority to approve code-sensitive substitutions, trade signoff, or permit readiness

domain_failure_modes: Materials are treated as available even though permit, inspection, or site-readiness records are incomplete.
Vendor or shortage pressure causes the lane to bypass spend approval, approved-vendor controls, or documented submittal states.
A substitution request with code or trade implications is handled as routine procurement instead of escalated to licensed authority.
Inventory is reported as ready without authoritative lot, serial, laydown, or delivery evidence.
Procurement completion is confused with inspection completion, closeout completion, or certificate-of-occupancy readiness.
Safety hold points or inspection comments are ignored because the lane focuses only on material timing.

scenario_family_coverage.scenario_family: Request that implies licensed trade or code-interpretation authority
Required supply is unavailable or short
Vendor or substitution decision exceeds documented authority
Traceability or inventory evidence is incomplete

unresolved_ambiguities.topic: Jurisdiction-specific permit classes, named inspections, and occupancy terminology
unresolved_ambiguities.notes: Public permitting workflow is authoritative only at the jurisdiction level. The pack therefore requires tenant or jurisdiction retrieval for exact permit classes, local hold points, and occupancy or closeout terms instead of guessing from a single city baseline.

unresolved_ambiguities.topic: Whether a given project is governed by public-grant procurement controls or private project controls
unresolved_ambiguities.notes: FEMA procurement guidance is authoritative when grant or public-funding rules apply. Private projects may follow contract-specific controls instead, so the lane must ask for funding and contract context before asserting procurement-control specifics.
